era_context: Egypt's Old Kingdom, Dynasty IV, likely in the reign of Khufu (c. 2589–2566 BCE, traditional dating) or the early years of his successor. The Giza pyramid complex is under active construction or refinement. In Mesopotamia, the Akkad Empire is not yet dominant; Sumerian city-states still hold power. The Indus Valley Civilisation is in its early urban phase. This is a period of high centralised power in Egypt and early state formation in the Indus.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesopotamia — no specific events attested for this year
Indus Valley — no written records; only archaeological inference
Sub-Saharan Africa, East Asia, Americas — no contemporary documentation

events.title: Continuation of Khufu's reign and Giza construction
events.description: If 2589 BCE marks Khufu's accession, 2588 BCE falls within his reign. The Giza plateau continued to be the focus of Egyptian state resources. The Great Pyramid itself was likely completed or nearing completion by this time (traditional dating c. 2560 BCE), but the broader complex—including the Pyramid of Khafre, the Sphinx, causeway temples, and subsidiary structures—was under development across multiple reigns. This year represents the continuation of the Old Kingdom's most ambitious architectural programme.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Giza; Abydos King List

events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for this year. The dating of Khufu's reign itself is uncertain by ±20–30 years. This entry is a placeholder reflecting the broader era context.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are attested for 2588 BCE. The uncertainty in Old Kingdom chronology means this year could fall in Khufu's reign, Khafre's reign, or even earlier/later depending on which chronological system is adopted. The 'low chronology' would place this year in a different reign entirely.
historiographic_note: For this year, there are no specific historical events recorded in any surviving source. The entry above is a contextual placeholder. The historical record for the Old Kingdom is almost entirely monumental and archaeological; narrative history is absent. This reflects both the nature of Egyptian record-keeping (focused on the eternal and the monumental, not the quotidian) and the loss of administrative records.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of same reign and construction programme
